title: Christian Kingdoms
subtitle: 718–1230 · Asturias to Las Navas de Tolosa

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: From mountain refuge to multi-kingdom Iberia

entries.label: Pelayo — founder of Asturias
entries.hint: Pelayo (Pelagius) founds the Kingdom of Asturias after defeating a Muslim force at Covadonga (c.722) — the battle is later magnified into the founding moment of the Reconquista; the earliest primary sources are from Alfonso III's court (866–910), 150 years later; Covadonga is as much myth as history.
entries.note: Chronicle of Alfonso III (c.910): primary source — but 150 years after the events, ideologically motivated. The Battle of Covadonga: dated c.718–722 in scholarship (Collins, Reconquista debate). Britannica: 'the stories and relics of Pelayo belong to legend rather than fact; it was in this legendary guise that he became an important symbol'. Linehan (1993), History and the Historians of Medieval Spain: critiques the source tradition.

entries.label: Alfonso II — the Chaste and Santiago de Compostela
entries.hint: Alfonso II builds the original Santiago de Compostela cathedral over the supposed tomb of St James discovered c.830 — establishes Oviedo as capital with its own church architecture; makes the first recorded pilgrimage to Santiago (813); the Asturian kingdom begins its southward expansion.
entries.note: Alfonso II's construction programme: documented in chronicles. The discovery of St James's tomb (c.830): documented in contemporary correspondence including a letter from Alfonso II to Charlemagne. Moralejo (1985) on Asturian architecture. The first pilgrimage: Kingdom of Asturias LibGuide (STLCC) documents Alfonso II's 813 pilgrimage as 'the first pilgrimage to Santiago de Compostela'.

entries.label: Alfonso VI — Toledo reconquered (1085)
entries.hint: Alfonso VI of León and Castile reconquers Toledo on 25 May 1085 — the first major Muslim city taken in the Reconquista, the old Visigothic capital; the Christian world celebrates; the shocked Muslim taifas invite the Almoravids from Morocco, who reverse many gains.
entries.note: The reconquest of Toledo 25 May 1085: documented in both Christian chronicles and Arabic sources. The Almoravid invitation: ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib. Alfonso VI's title 'Emperor of all Spain': documented in royal charters (Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo). El Cid's relationship with Alfonso VI: documented in the Chronica Adefonsi Imperatoris. Barton & Fletcher (2000), The World of El Cid.

entries.label: Alfonso VIII — Las Navas de Tolosa (1212)
entries.hint: Alfonso VIII leads a coalition of Castile, Aragon, Navarre and Portugal to decisive victory over the Almohad Caliphate at Las Navas de Tolosa on 16 July 1212 — the turning point of the Reconquista; within 40 years Córdoba, Seville and Valencia fall; the Almohad empire collapses.
entries.note: The battle 16 Jul 1212: documented in Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada, De Rebus Hispaniae (c.1243) — primary Castilian source; he was present. Arabic sources: Ibn Abi Zar, Rawdh al-Qirtas. Alfonso VIII's correspondence with Pope Innocent III requesting a crusade: Vatican Archives. Lomax (1978), The Reconquista of Spain: the standard English study.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: The Reconquista — more complex than the legend

entries.label: Battle of Covadonga — the founding myth
entries.hint: Pelayo's victory over a Muslim force at Covadonga — the scale is disputed (the Chronicle of 754, written by a Christian under Muslim rule, calls it a minor skirmish; later Asturian chronicles inflate it enormously); the gap between the historical event and the national myth is itself a primary historical fact.
entries.note: Chronicle of 754 (primary, most contemporary): describes a minor engagement. Chronicle of Alfonso III (c.910): inflates it as a providential founding. Britannica's assessment: 'Rather than a single engagement, Covadonga can be seen as the final act in a series of rebellions starting in 718.' Collins (1989), The Arab Conquest of Spain: analyses the source discrepancy. The shrine at Covadonga: extant — the physical monument to the mythologisation.

entries.label: El Cid takes Valencia (1094)
entries.hint: Rodrigo Díaz de Vivar (El Cid) captures Valencia in June 1094 and rules it independently until his death in 1099 — a Christian warrior sometimes fighting for Muslim rulers against Christians; his career is the clearest example of the Reconquista's complexity; his widow Jimena holds Valencia two more years.
entries.note: Carmen Campidoctoris (c.1090s): the earliest Latin poem about El Cid — near-contemporary evidence. Historia Roderici (c.1110): the primary Latin biography. The Cantar de Mio Cid (c.1207): the literary account — manuscript Biblioteca Nacional de España, Vitr.7-17. Fletcher (1989), The Quest for El Cid: uses the Historia Roderici as the primary source. Valencia falls back to the Almoravids 1102 — three years after El Cid's death.

entries.label: Battle of Las Navas de Tolosa — the turning point
entries.loc: Las Navas de Tolosa, Jaén (Sierra Morena)
entries.hint: The combined Christian kingdoms defeat the Almohad Caliph al-Nasir — estimates of dead range from 2,000 to 100,000 (sources vary wildly); within a generation the Almohad empire collapses; the battle opens Andalusia to Christian reconquest.
entries.note: Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada, De Rebus Hispaniae (c.1243): eyewitness account — he was present and coordinated the coalition. Pope Innocent III's letter of congratulation: Vatican Archives. Arabic sources: Ibn Abi Zar. Casualty figures: impossible to verify — the wildly varying numbers document medieval tendencies in military reporting. The captured Almohad standard: reportedly sent to Las Huelgas monastery, Burgos (where it survives).

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Fueros, royal charters and the development of Iberian law

entries.label: Fueros — the municipal charters of Christian Spain
entries.hint: The fueros (municipal charters) granted to repopulated frontier towns from the 11th century onwards give towns significant autonomy and legal rights — the Fuero of León (1017/1020) is among the earliest; they are the primary legal evidence for the organisation of Reconquista society.
entries.note: Fuero of León (1017/1020): Archivo de la Catedral de León — primary text, digitised. Fuero de Jaca (c.1063): also early. García de Valdeavellano (1968), Historia de España Vol.I: the standard survey of the fuero system. The fueros' long-term importance: the Cádiz Constitution debates (1810) explicitly invoke the fuero tradition. They are among the earliest documents of representative governance in Western Europe.

entries.label: Santiago de Compostela Cathedral — the Romanesque primary source
entries.hint: Construction of the Cathedral of Santiago de Compostela begins in 1075 under Bishop Diego Peláez and the Pilgrim's Guide (c.1139) is the first European tourist guide — documenting the pilgrimage routes, shrines and hostels; the Codex Calixtinus (c.1160) contains the earliest polyphonic Western music.
entries.note: UNESCO World Heritage (1985). Building begun 1075: documented in the Chronicon Compostellanum. Pilgrim's Guide (Liber V of Codex Calixtinus, c.1139): BnF and Archivo de la Catedral de Santiago — the first European travel guide. The Codex Calixtinus (c.1160): Archivo de la Catedral de Santiago — stolen 2011, recovered 2012. Moralejo et al. (1992), Liber Sancti Jacobi: the standard edition.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: The pilgrimage, the military orders and the Iberian Church

entries.label: The Camino de Santiago — pilgrimage as geopolitical act
entries.loc: Routes from France through Iberia to Santiago de Compostela
entries.hint: The discovery of St James's tomb c.830 creates the most important pilgrimage route in medieval Europe — by the 11th century 500,000 pilgrims annually; the route brings French Romanesque architecture, monastic reform (Cluny), new settlement patterns and pan-European cultural contact.
entries.note: The discovery narrative: Alfonso II's letter (c.830). The Pilgrim's Guide c.1139: first documented tourist guide in Europe (Liber V of Codex Calixtinus). Pilgrimage numbers: Valiña Sampedro (1971) estimates. The Cluniac reform connection: documented in papal correspondence (Vatican Archives). The route's physical evidence: documented archaeological finds along the Camino. UNESCO World Heritage (1993).

entries.label: Military orders — knights of Santiago, Calatrava, Alcántara
entries.hint: Three Iberian military orders are founded in 60 years — the Order of Calatrava (1158), Order of Santiago (1170) and Order of Alcántara (c.1176); they combine monastery and castle, hold frontier zones, and become major landowners; their archives are primary sources for the Reconquista's social organisation.
entries.note: Order of Calatrava: founded by Abbot Raimundo of Fitero (1158), confirmed by Pope Alexander III (1164) — papal bull in Vatican Archives. Order of Santiago: papal confirmation 1175 — Vatican Archives. O'Callaghan (1975), The Spanish Military Order of Calatrava: the standard study. The orders' archives: Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid (Sección de Órdenes Militares).

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Romanesque art, the Cantar de Mio Cid and Mozarab manuscripts

entries.label: Asturian pre-Romanesque — Santa María del Naranco (848)
entries.hint: Santa María del Naranco (848) is the finest surviving pre-Romanesque building in Spain — originally Alfonso I's palace, converted to a church; its barrel vaults, blind arcading and elevated siting anticipate Romanesque; it survives because it became a church.
entries.note: Extant (UNESCO 1985 as part of 'Monuments of Oviedo'). Built 842–848 under Ramiro I: documented in contemporary chronicles. The palace-to-church conversion: documented. Schlunk & Berenguer (1957), La pintura mural asturiana: the standard architectural study. The building's survival: one of the very few 9th-century secular buildings surviving in Western Europe.

entries.label: Cantar de Mio Cid — the first Spanish epic
entries.loc: San Pedro de Cardeña monastery (transmission); Burgos
entries.hint: The Cantar de Mio Cid is the earliest surviving major literary text in Castilian Spanish — the single surviving manuscript is dated c.1207; the poem depicts El Cid fighting Muslims and Christians alike, arranging his daughters' marriages; it is the primary literary evidence for 12th-century Castilian values.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Manuscript Per Abbat (1207): Biblioteca Nacional de España, Vitr.7-17 — the single surviving medieval manuscript. Published: Menéndez Pidal (1908, 1944): the standard critical editions. Smith (1983), The Making of the Poema de Mio Cid: the standard English study. The manuscript's provenance: from the monastery of San Pedro de Cardeña. The poem's historical reliability: it post-dates El Cid by ~100 years.

entries.label: Codex Calixtinus — first polyphony and the pilgrimage songs
entries.loc: Santiago de Compostela (Archivo de la Catedral)
entries.hint: The Codex Calixtinus (c.1160) contains 20 polyphonic compositions — the earliest dated polyphonic Western music; it also contains the Pilgrim's Guide and liturgical chants for the Santiago pilgrimage; stolen from the cathedral in 2011, recovered 2012.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Codex Calixtinus (c.1160): Archivo de la Catedral de Santiago de Compostela — returned after theft and recovery 2011–2012. Moralejo et al. (1992), Liber Sancti Jacobi (Codex Calixtinus): critical edition. The polyphonic compositions: identified as the earliest dated polyphony in Western music by musicologists. Stones (2011), A Manuscript of the Codex Calixtinus: the standard musicological study.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: The Toledo school of translators — Arabic to Latin

entries.label: Toledo school of translators — Arabic science reaches Europe
entries.hint: After Toledo's reconquest (1085) a wave of European scholars come to translate Arabic philosophy and science into Latin — Aristotle, Ptolemy, Avicenna, al-Zahrawi, al-Khwarizmi; Gerard of Cremona alone translates 87 works; this transmission is how ancient Greek thought reaches medieval Europe.
entries.note: Gerard of Cremona (c.1114–1187): translated 87 Arabic works including Ptolemy's Almagest — documented in his obituary notice by his pupils (published Burnett 2001). Adelard of Bath's translations (c.1116–1142): documented. Burnett (2001), 'The Coherence of the Arabic-Latin Translation Program in Toledo' in Science in Context 14: the standard modern study. The physical manuscripts of translations: BnF and British Library hold early copies.

entries.label: Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada — De Rebus Hispaniae
entries.hint: Archbishop Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada's De Rebus Hispaniae (On the Affairs of Spain, c.1243) is the first comprehensive history of the Iberian Peninsula — he was present at Las Navas de Tolosa (1212) and his account of it is an eyewitness document; the work defines the Reconquista as a providential national narrative.
entries.note: De Rebus Hispaniae (c.1243): manuscripts in multiple archives; printed edition Lorenzana (1793). Rodrigo's eyewitness account of Las Navas de Tolosa: the most detailed contemporary narrative. Linehan (1993), History and the Historians of Medieval Spain: the standard critical study of the source tradition. The work's 'national narrative' framing: Linehan argues it retrospectively constructs a Spanish identity.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Repopulation, frontier economy and the Camino's commercial impact

entries.label: Repopulation — the demographic engine of the Reconquista
entries.hint: The Christian kingdoms systematically repopulate conquered territory — granting fueros to attract settlers to dangerous frontier zones; the 10th–12th century sees the Duero valley, then the Tagus valley, then Extremadura settled; repopulation documents are primary sources for medieval demographic and economic history.
entries.note: Repopulation charters (cartas de población): Archivo de la Catedral de León; Archivo Histórico Nacional. The fuero system's role in repopulation: documented in the charters. Powers (1988), A Society Organized for War: The Iberian Municipal Militias: the standard study of frontier society. The population movement's scale: documented in charter density maps by Barton (2004), The Aristocracy in Twelfth-Century León and Castile.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: The Camino as cultural highway and Toledo as translation hub

entries.label: Cluny and the Camino — French monasticism transforms Iberia
entries.loc: Cluniac monasteries along the Camino; Burgos; León
entries.hint: The Cluny monastery reforms spread along the Camino — Alfonso VI invites Cluniac monks to reform Spanish monasteries; French Romanesque architecture arrives with the pilgrims; the French Way brings not just pilgrims but settlers, craftsmen and a new European cultural contact.
entries.note: Alfonso VI's Cluny donations: documented in Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo and Cluny's own archives. The Romanesque architecture spread: documented in the Pilgrim's Guide c.1139. Bishko (1969), 'Fernando I and the Origins of the Leonese-Castilian Alliance with Cluny': the standard study. The broader cultural exchange: documented in the building programme at Santiago Cathedral.

entries.label: Christian-Muslim alliances — the Reconquista's real complexity
entries.hint: Christian and Muslim rulers regularly ally against their co-religionists — El Cid fights for the Muslim king of Zaragoza, Alfonso VI makes alliances with Muslim taifas, Castilian and Aragonese kings negotiate with Almohads; the Reconquista is as much politics as religion.
entries.note: El Cid's service to the Muslim king of Zaragoza: Historia Roderici (c.1110) — primary source. The parias system (tribute paid by taifas to Christian kings): documented in royal archives. Fletcher (1989), The Quest for El Cid, ch.5–6. The cross-religious alliances: O'Callaghan (2003), Reconquest and Crusade in Medieval Spain — argues the religious framing was always mixed with political calculation.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: The Iberian kingdoms in European and Mediterranean context

entries.label: The Almoravids and Almohads — North Africa intervenes
entries.hint: The fragmented taifa kingdoms invite the Almoravid Berbers from Morocco in 1086 — they reverse Christian gains and subjugate the taifas; then the even stricter Almohad movement succeeds them; the North African interventions transform al-Andalus and eventually provoke the coalition that wins at Las Navas.
entries.note: The Almoravid invitation 1086: Ibn Idhari, al-Bayan al-Mughrib. The Almohad invasion 1147: documented in Arabic sources. The Battle of Alarcos (1195) — Almohad victory over Alfonso VIII: documented in De Rebus Hispaniae. Lomax (1978), The Reconquista of Spain: the standard survey. The paradox: the taifas' invitation of the Almoravids was a strategic mistake — the Almoravids quickly deposed the taifa rulers they came to protect.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Queens regent and 'reinas propietarias' — ruling women of medieval Iberia
entries.hint: Medieval Iberia produces a remarkable number of ruling queens — Urraca of León (1109–1126) is the first queen regnant of a major medieval European kingdom to rule in her own right, without a male co-ruler; her charters document executive authority clearly.
entries.note: Queen Urraca's charters (1109–1126): Archivo de la Catedral de León and Archivo Histórico Nacional — multiple charters in her own name. Reilly (1982), The Kingdom of León-Castilla under Queen Urraca 1109–1126: the standard biography. The title 'regina totius Hispaniae': documented in her charters. Iberian queens regnant before the 14th century: Urraca, Berenguela (Castile), Teresa (Portugal) — all documented through their own acts.

entries.label: Jews under Christian rule — fueros and protection
entries.loc: Toledo; Burgos; León; Barcelona (main Jewish communities)
entries.hint: The Jewish communities of Christian Iberia live under royal protection — the fuero system grants them specific rights; Alfonso VI's Toledo is deliberately multi-religious after the reconquest (1085); the community produces scholars in both Hebrew and Arabic, connecting the Sephardic world to European learning.
entries.note: Alfonso VI's Toledo charters for Jews (post-1085): Archivo de la Catedral de Toledo. The Barcelona Jewish community: documented in municipal records from 10th century. Baer (1961–1966), A History of the Jews in Christian Spain (2 vols): the standard study. The contrast with Visigothic persecution: a genuine improvement documented in the charters. The vulnerability before the 1391 pogroms: foreshadowed in the 1109 Toledo pogrom after Alfonso VI's death.

events.l: Las Navas de Tolosa — turning point
Rodrigo Jiménez de Rada — De Rebus Hispaniae
Urraca — first queen regnant in Europe
